content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • S£lcrnmcnto, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Juan Garza 
Bellflower City Councilmember 
16600 Civic Center Drive 
Bellflower, CA 90706 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17207 
Dear Mr. Garza: 
September 28, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest or Section l 090. Please also note that we are not a finder of 
fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) l FPPC Ops. 71), and that any advice we provide 
assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit you from taking part in governmental decisions relating to the City of 
Bellflower's proposed Outdoor Advertising Development Plan, which grants an exclusive 
development agreement to Bulletin Displays, LLC ("Bulletin Displays") to execute the Plan, given 
that you are a Vice President at Englander Knabe & Allen and that that firm has two clients that are 
competitors of Bulletin Displays? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to the proposed Plan 
because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your 
financial interest in Englander Knabe & Allen as a source of income.2 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references arc to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 A public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 and who has a disqualifying conflict of 
interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting must: (I) publicly identify each financial interest that gives rise to the 
disqualifying conflict of interest, (2) recuse himself or herself from the decision, and (3) leave the room for the duration 
of the discussion and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 18707 .) Because you arc a city council member 
and that office is specified in Section 87200, and because you have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision 
noticed at a public meeting relating to the proposed Plan, you must comply with the rccusal requirements of Section 
87105 and Regulation 18707 with respect to that decision. 


FACTS 
File No. A-17-207 
Page No. 2 
You are a City of Bellflower City Councilmember. In your private capacity, you are a Vice 
President at Englander Knabe & Allen. According to its website, Englander Knabe & Allen "is a 
strategic communications firm specializing in government affairs, communications, litigation 
support, and issues management." You receive a salary from Englander Knabe & Allen for your 
work as a Vice President, and you have received more than $500 from the firm within the 12 
months prior to the decisions at issue. 
The City Council will soon consider whether to approve a proposed Outdoor Advertising 
Development Plan. The Plan would authorize five double-sided outdoor advertising signs to be built 
within the City, two on surface streets and three on the 91 Freeway, and would grant an exclusive 
development agreement to Bulletin Displays to execute the Plan. The Plan authorizes new digital 
outdoor advertising signs, and generally does not affect existing static outdoor advertising signs. 
Englander Knabe & Allen currently has two clients that compete with Bulletin Displays for 
outdoor advertising business, and one of those clients is your client. Clear Channel Outdoor 
Holdings, Inc. ("Clear Channel") is one of Englander Knabe & Allen's two clients that are 
competitors of Bulletin Displays. Englander Knabe & Allen's website indicates that only partners, 
senior vice presidents, or vice presidents provide strategy and implementation to clients on its 
behalf. 
Under the proposed Plan, Clear Channel is tasked with reviewing the fifth potential digital 
outdoor advertising sign location within the City and determining whether it would like to be 
included within the Plan. However, you confirmed that Clear Channel is not interested in 
participating in the Plan or having a digital outdoor advertising sign anywhere within the City at 
this time. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in the 
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in 
which the official has a financial interest.3 Section 87103 provides that a public official has a 
"financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified 
in that section. Of the interests identified in Section 87103, the decisions at issue and the facts 
presented implicate the following: 
3 For purposes of the Act, a public official: "makes a decision" if the official authorizes or directs any action, 
votes, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency lo any course of action, or enters into any contractual 
agreement on behalf of his or her agency (Regulation l8704(a)); "participates in a decision" if the official provides 
information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the purpose of affecting the decision without significant intervening 
substantive review (Regulation l 8704(b )); and "uses official position to attempt to influence a decision" if the official 
( l) contacts or appears before any official in his or her agency or in an agency subject to the authority or budgetary 
control of his or her agency for the purpose of affecting a decision or (2) contacts or appears before any official in any 
other government agency for the purpose of affecting a decision, and the official acts or purports to act within his or her 
authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making the contact (Regulation l 8704(c)). 


File No. A-17-207 
Page No. 3 
• 
Any business entity in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or 
holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)). You have a business interest in 
Englander Knabe & Allen because, as a Vice President, you hold a management position with 
the firm. 
• 
Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the official within t 2 
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) You have a source-of-income interest in 
Englander Knabe & Allen because you have aggregated $500 or more in income from the finn 
within the 12 months preceding the decisions at issue. 
• 
An interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate family members. (See Section 
87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A decision's financial effect on an official's financial interest is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 1870l(a).) 
An interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, 
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. 
(Ibid.) If the interest is not a named party in or the subject of the decision, the decision's financial 
effect is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) 
Neither Englander Knabe & Allen nor your personal finances are a named party in, or the 
subject of, the decisions at issue. Therefore, those decisions' financial effect on your interests in 
either that firm or your personal finances is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
Regulation 18702.3 provides the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on an 
official's source of income. Subdivision (c) of that regulation sets forth the "Nexus Test," which 
provides that "[a]ny reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of income 
to a public official is deemed material if the public official receives or is promised the income to 
achieve a goal or purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision." 
The rationale for the Nexus Test is that when an employee earns a salary to accomplish a 
purpose that may be advanced by what he or she does as a public official, we presume that the 
private employer is benefiting from the actions of the employee in his or her official capacity. (Tran 
Advice Letter, No. A-16-024; Maltbie Advice Letter, No. A-15-243.) Typically, a "nexus" is found 
in situations where the official is also a high-level employee with direct influence and control over 
his or her employer's management or policy decisions. (Tran Advice Letter, No. A-16-024; 
Moser Advice Letter, No. A-03-147; Low Advice Letter, No. A-99-305.) 
The proposed Plan will have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Englander Knabe 
& Allen because its clients are competitors of Bulletin Displays, those clients' interests will not be 
served by Bulletin Displays' potential exclusive development agreement to implement the proposed 


File No. A-17-207 
Page No. 4 
Plan, and those clients may be less likely to conduct additional business with Englander Knabe & 
Allen if their interests are not being advanced by the firm's management. You earn a salary from 
Englander Knabe & Allen to advance its clients' interests, those relating to government affairs in 
particular. As a Vice President, you are part of an exclusive group of the Englander Knabe & 
Allen's managers that provides strategy and implementation recommendations to the firm's clients. 
Because the decisions relating to the Plan would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on 
Englander Knabe & Allen, and because you a high-level employee of that firm with direct influence 
and control over its management or policy decisions, there is an impermissible nexus between the 
decisions relating to the Plan and your interest in that firm as your private employer. Therefore, the 
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the decisions relating to the Plan on your source-of­
income interest in Englander Knabe & Allen is material under the Nexus Test. 
Thus, the Act prohibits you from taking part in those decisions. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Jack C. Woodside 
General Counsel 
- 
By: 
Matthew . Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division 
4 We do not analyze the financial effect of the decisions relating to the Plan on your other financial interests 
because we have already detennined that the Act prohibits you from taking part in those decisions.

Yes. The Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to the proposed Plan 
because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your 
financial interest in Englander Knabe & Allen as a source of income.22 A public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 and who has a disqualifying conflict of 
interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting must: (I) publicly identify each financial interest that gives rise to the

87105 and Regulation 18707 with respect to that decision.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in the 
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in 
which the official has a financial interest.3 Section 87103 provides that a public official has a 
"financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified 
in that section. Of the interests identified in Section 87103, the decisions at issue and the facts 
presented implicate the following: 
3 For purposes of the Act, a public official: "makes a decision" if the official authorizes or directs any action, 
votes, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency lo any course of action, or enters into any contractual 
agreement on behalf of his or her agency (Regulation l8704(a)); "participates in a decision" if the official provides 
information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the purpose of affecting the decision without significant intervening 
substantive review (Regulation l 8704(b )); and "uses official position to attempt to influence a decision" if the official 
( l) contacts or appears before any official in his or her agency or in an agency subject to the authority or budgetary 
control of his or her agency for the purpose of affecting a decision or (2) contacts or appears before any official in any 
other government agency for the purpose of affecting a decision, and the official acts or purports to act within his or her 
authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making the contact (Regulation l 8704(c)).  
• 
Any business entity in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or 
holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)). You have a business interest in 
Englander Knabe & Allen because, as a Vice President, you hold a management position with 
the firm. 
• 
Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the official within t 2 
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) You have a source-of-income interest in 
Englander Knabe & Allen because you have aggregated $500 or more in income from the finn 
within the 12 months preceding the decisions at issue. 
• 
An interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate family members. (See Section 
87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A decision's financial effect on an official's financial interest is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 1870l(a).) 
An interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, 
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. 
(Ibid.) If the interest is not a named party in or the subject of the decision, the decision's financial 
effect is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) 
Neither Englander Knabe & Allen nor your personal finances are a named party in, or the 
subject of, the decisions at issue. Therefore, those decisions' financial effect on your interests in 
either that firm or your personal finances is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
Regulation 18702.3 provides the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on an 
official's source of income. Subdivision (c) of that regulation sets forth the "Nexus Test," which

The rationale for the Nexus Test is that when an employee earns a salary to accomplish a 
purpose that may be advanced by what he or she does as a public official, we presume that the 
private employer is benefiting from the actions of the employee in his or her official capacity. (Tran

in situations where the official is also a high-level employee with direct influence and control over 
his or her employer's management or policy decisions. (Tran Advice Letter, No. A-16-024; 
Moser Advice Letter, No. A-03-147; Low Advice Letter, No. A-99-305.) 
The proposed Plan will have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Englander Knabe 
& Allen because its clients are competitors of Bulletin Displays, those clients' interests will not be 
served by Bulletin Displays' potential exclusive development agreement to implement the proposed  
Plan, and those clients may be less likely to conduct additional business with Englander Knabe & 
Allen if their interests are not being advanced by the firm's management. You earn a salary from 
Englander Knabe & Allen to advance its clients' interests, those relating to government affairs in 
particular. As a Vice President, you are part of an exclusive group of the Englander Knabe & 
Allen's managers that provides strategy and implementation recommendations to the firm's clients. 
Because the decisions relating to the Plan would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on 
Englander Knabe & Allen, and because you a high-level employee of that firm with direct influence 
and control over its management or policy decisions, there is an impermissible nexus between the 
decisions relating to the Plan and your interest in that firm as your private employer. Therefore, the 
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the decisions relating to the Plan on your source-of­
income interest in Englander Knabe & Allen is material under the Nexus Test. 
Thus, the Act prohibits you from taking part in those decisions. 4
